channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Can the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire Survive Days of Strikes?
item.description: Analysts saw little prospect of either President Trump or Tehran backing down after days of exchanging fire.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump has pushed for a peace deal while exchanging airstrikes with Iran.

item.title: Trump and Hegseth Broadcast U.S. Military Strikes Before They Happen
item.description: Commanders do not usually speak publicly about future operations to avoid jeopardizing the mission’s success.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump said he was telegraphing the American strikes to pressure Iran to reach a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Trump Administration Pushes Limits of Election Investigations
item.description: The Justice Department once tried to stay out of state elections, urging caution. It is now pressing forward with claims of fraud as President Trump revives his unfounded assertions that elections cannot be trusted.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters casting their ballots in California’s primary races in Los Angeles last week.

item.title: House Rejects Bill to Extend Surveillance Power With FISA Section 702 Set to Expire
item.description: A measure to temporarily continue a key surveillance law fell well short of the support needed to pass, further raising the chances that it will lapse on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We cannot allow that to go dark,” Speaker Mike Johnson told reporters on Wednesday as prospects for the law being renewed appeared increasingly grim.

item.title: With Measles Roaring Back, the Search for a Drug is On
item.description: As vaccination rates decline, biotech companies see a potential new market for measles drugs.
item.dc:creator: Teddy Rosenbluth
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs via Getty

item.title: 30 Shows to Watch This Summer
item.description: “The Bear” serves its last course, “Ted Lasso” gets back on the pitch, and Larry David makes a TV show with the Obamas.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: “Ted Lasso” returns in August with, center, from left, Brendan Hunt, Jason Sudeikis and Tanya Reynolds.

item.title: Hazardous Temperatures Forecast for Many World Cup Games
item.description: Many of the tournament’s host cities risk hitting dangerous heat levels this year, raising questions about the safety of players and fans.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:description: Thiago Santos, a player with Brazil’s Fluminense team, during a cooling break at a match in New Jersey last July.

item.title: The War in Ukraine Has Now Gone On Longer Than World War I
item.description: Parallels between the two wars abound, from the grinding nature of the fighting to the way new technologies reshaped warfare.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: A Ukrainian soldier in Kostiantynivka, a city ravaged by drone warfare, in January.

item.title: Can’t Pay Medical Bills? Trump Administration Suggests Getting a Loan
item.description: One-third of Americans shoulder health care debt. Insurers are being asked to consider lending money to Obamacare consumers who can’t afford higher deductibles.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.category: Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Cig Harvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kathleen Capetta’s family of five is paying an additional $750 a month this year for its Obamacare plan premium. She was already paying off debt from cancer treatment.

item.title: In Britain, a Violent Cycle: Hateful Attacks, Right-Wing Agitation and Riots
item.description: Two stabbings have fueled violent protests in England and Northern Ireland in the last 10 days, fanned online by right-wing voices. In Britain and across Europe, it’s a grim pattern.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Protests north of Belfast, Northern Ireland, on Wednesday in response to a stabbing attack two days earlier.

item.title: The Researcher Who Didn’t Want to Know
item.description: Her decades of work on Huntington’s disease helped lead to the creation of a genetic test for the devastating condition. Why didn’t she take it herself?
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nancy Wexler a lifelong researcher of Huntington’s disease, who has the condition herself, in her Manhattan apartment.

item.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A talk on rising energy costs on Capitol Hill hosted by Democrats, from left, Sen. Martin Heinrich of New Mexico, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, in March.

item.title: These Young Politicians Want to Fix America’s Housing Problems
item.description: A cohort of young Millennial and Gen Z politicians have centered their campaigns this year on housing costs, and the divide is more generational than partisan.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Morales, 27, a candidate for mayor of Providence, R.I., has made housing central to his pitch for “a city all our neighbors can afford.”

item.title: Conor McGregor’s Comeback: A Tale of Banned Drugs and a Famous Doctor
item.description: McGregor, the Ultimate Fighting Championship’s main attraction, had the support of the prominent sports physician Neal ElAttrache when he decided to use performance-enhancing drugs.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:description: Conor McGregor preparing to fight Dustin Poirier in Las Vegas in 2021. McGregor suffered a badly broken leg during the bout.

item.title: The Joy of Missing Out on SpaceX’s I.P.O.
item.description: You’re not important or lucky enough to have access to shares at the offering price, and that’s OK. Here’s why.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber

item.title: Israel Set to Rapidly Expand West Bank Settlement
item.description: In a major push, the government is rushing to place temporary housing at about 60 empty sites in the Israeli-occupied West Bank before national elections this fall.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:description: New prefabricated outpost settlement homes in May near the occupied West Bank village of Umm al-Khair and the Israeli settlement of Karmel on the hill.

item.title: Why It’s Nearly Impossible to Build a Robot Without China
item.description: Building on the country’s electric vehicle industry, Chinese companies are making robot parts at a scale and price point others can’t match.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times

item.title: Jeff Bezos Wants to Build an ‘Artificial General Engineer’
item.description: As co-chief executive of the start-up Prometheus, the Amazon founder is using A.I. to improve how devices ranging from computers to jet engines are made.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: With his new company, Prometheus, Jeff Bezos is training A.I. on the design and manufacturing process of various industries.

item.title: Inside Trump’s White House, the Epstein Files Caused a Freakout
item.description: The president’s top advisers gathered in a series of Situation Room meetings as they struggled to contain a scandal engulfing Donald Trump himself.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump (Book)

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Sex, Lies and Secrets: A Federal Judge’s Trysts Go Public
item.description: Now, Judge Eleanor Ross’s career and caseload are under scrutiny. And her punishment, a private reprimand, has sparked backlash.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:description: Judge Eleanor Ross during a congressional hearing in 2014.

item.title: She Was One of Obama’s Top Lawyers. How Did She Get Tangled Up With Epstein?
item.description: That she, of all people, looked past his 2008 conviction tells you everything you need to know about how unreliable and corroded the legal world has become.
item.dc:creator: Ankush Khardori
item.media:credit: Cait Oppermann for The New York Times

item.title: The Terrible Man Theory of History
item.description: The solution to Trumpism isn’t to conform to the spirit of the age, but to transform it.
item.dc:creator: David French

item.title: The World Cup
item.description: Here’s what you should know about this massive competition.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Desi Lydic Does a Spit Take at the Price of Trump’s Gold Coin
item.description: “$12,000? For a coin? Does it come with a used Honda Civic?” Lydic said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: A Newer Approach to Editing Embryos Ignites Debate
item.description: Fertility specialists, biotech companies and ethicists are divided over whether progress in early gene editing would wipe out diseases or trigger a rush toward enhancement.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:description: A colored light micrograph of a human embryo 48 to 72 hours after in vitro fertilization. “Human embryos become living human persons,” one bioethicist said. “The research is being done on future persons without their consent.”
